criteria: The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the core architecture of RidgeLoRA, specifically the series connection formulation replacing vanilla LoRA's parallel connection.
The agent has read Equation 1 to understand the RidgeLoRA forward computation: X_out = X_in(λΣ + α/r AB)W, where Σ is a learnable diagonal ridge matrix.
The agent has read Equation 2 to understand the weight absorption formula: W' = (λΣ + α/r AB)W, enabling inference without additional overhead.
The agent has read Section 3.3 and Algorithm 1 completely to understand the weight initialization strategy of RidgeLoRA.
The agent has read Equations 4 and 5 to understand the Ridge Squashing Loss and its integration into the total training loss.
The agent has read Section 4.1 completely to understand the experimental setup including datasets, baselines, optimizer, learning rates, and rank settings.
The agent has read Table 2 to understand the commonsense reasoning evaluation results across eight datasets with three base models.
The agent has read Table 3 to understand the math and code problem evaluation results across four benchmarks.
The agent has read Table 4 to understand the multi-modal understanding evaluation results on four vision-language tasks.
The agent has read Tables 5 and 6 to understand the ablation study on Ridge-enhanced parallel connections vs. series connections.
The agent has read Tables 7 and 8 to understand the ablation study on weight initialization methods and β_RS hyperparameter selection.
The agent has read Table 10 in Appendix B.2 to understand the NLU evaluation results on GLUE benchmark with BERT-like encoder models.
The plan includes implementing the RidgeLoRA forward pass as a series connection: X_out = X_in(λΣ + α/r AB)W, with trainable λ, Σ, A, and B.

The plan includes the weight initialization procedure from Algorithm 1: initializing λ=1, constructing Σ as a block-diagonal matrix, initializing A with Gaussian noise, and computing B via SVD to satisfy the transform-calibrating restriction.
The plan includes implementing the Ridge Squashing Loss L_RS as the average nuclear norm of (λΣ + α/r AB - I) across layers and blocks, fused with the task loss using a negative β_RS.
The plan includes loading all training and evaluation datasets for commonsense reasoning: BoolQ, PIQA, SocialIQA, HellaSwag, WinoGrande, ARC-Easy, ARC-Challenge, and OpenbookQA.
The plan includes loading datasets for math tasks (MetaMath for training, GSM8K and MATH for evaluation) and code tasks (Code-Feedback for training, HumanEval and MBPP for evaluation).
The plan includes implementing all baseline LoRA variants: vanilla LoRA, DoRA, PiSSA, and KaSA, alongside FFT as an upper bound.
The plan includes loading three base LLM models: Llama-2-7B (MHA), Llama-3.1-8B (GQA), and Mistral-v0.3-7B (GQA).
The plan includes the ablation study for Ridge-enhanced parallel connections by adding the Ridge term to LoRA, DoRA, PiSSA, and KaSA without changing their connection structure.
The plan includes the ablation study for different weight initialization methods: Diagonal, Gaussian (default), Kaiming (Normal and Uniform), and Xavier (Normal and Uniform).
The plan includes the ablation study for different β_RS values (both positive and negative) to validate the effect of the Ridge Squashing Loss.
The plan includes the NLU experiments using BERT-like models (RoBERTa-base, RoBERTa-large, DeBERTa-v3-base, DeBERTa-v3-large) on the GLUE benchmark.
The RidgeLoRA module has been implemented using a series connection: X_out = X_in(λΣ + α/r AB)W, replacing the parallel connection used in vanilla LoRA.
The diagonal ridge matrix Σ is implemented as a learnable d_in × d_in diagonal matrix with d_in trainable parameters on its diagonal, with full rank d_in.
The ridge intensity λ is implemented as a learnable scalar parameter initialized to 1.
The noise vector N for initializing the ridge diagonal is implemented as a sigmoid-transformed Gaussian sample: N = σ(N(μ_N, σ_N²)).
The ridge matrix Σ is initialized as a block-diagonal matrix [[diag(N), 0], [0, I_{d_in-r}]] where the top-left r×r block is diag(N) and the remaining block is an identity matrix.
Matrix A is initialized with Gaussian noise N(0, 1/r), followed by SVD decomposition to obtain U_A, Σ_A, V_A for computing the corresponding B initialization.
Matrix B is initialized to satisfy the transform-calibrating restriction (λΣ + α/r AB = I) using the formula B = r/α * V_A * Σ_A^{-1} * U_A^T * (I_{d_in} - λΣ).
The weight absorption mechanism is implemented: during inference, W' = (λΣ + α/r AB)W merges the trained parameters back into the original weight matrix.
The Ridge Squashing Loss L_RS is implemented as the average nuclear norm of (λΣ + α/r AB - I) summed over all L layers and B matrix blocks per layer.
The combined training loss is implemented as L = L_model + β_RS * L_RS, where β_RS is a hyperparameter set to a negative value (default -5×10^{-1}).
The AdamW optimizer is implemented with a cosine learning rate schedule for all training experiments.
The learning rate is set to 2×10^{-5} for Math & Code tasks and 3×10^{-5} for Commonsense Reasoning tasks, shared across all LoRA variants for fair comparison.
The rank of the low-rank matrices is set to 64 for Math & Code tasks and 8 for Commonsense Reasoning tasks.
The commonsense reasoning training uses multi-task training with the training splits of all eight datasets: BoolQ, PIQA, SocialIQA, HellaSwag, WinoGrande, ARC-Easy, ARC-Challenge, and OpenbookQA.
The math task training uses MetaMath as the training dataset, and evaluation is performed on GSM8K and MATH benchmarks.
The code task training uses Code-Feedback as the training dataset, and evaluation is performed on HumanEval and MBPP benchmarks (including the enhanced + variants).
The multi-modal understanding experiments use vicuna-7b-1.5 as the language model with a CLIP-based vision projector from LLaVA, following DoRA's configuration.
The baseline LoRA method is implemented with the standard parallel connection: X_out = X_in(W + α/r AB), matching the parameter count reported in Table 2.
The baseline DoRA method is implemented with weight decomposition into magnitude and direction factors, with an additional d_out scaling parameters per matrix.
The baseline PiSSA method is implemented using SVD on the original weight matrices to initialize the low-rank matrices with principal singular values and vectors.
The baseline KaSA method is implemented using SVD-based initialization with knowledge-aware singular value scaling A×Σ_r×B.
For the ablation study on Ridge-enhanced parallel connections, the Ridge term is added to LoRA, DoRA, PiSSA, and KaSA while keeping their original parallel connection structure intact.

For the weight initialization ablation, alternative initialization methods for matrix A are implemented: Diagonal (only diagonal values), Kaiming Normal, Kaiming Uniform, Xavier Normal, and Xavier Uniform.

For the β_RS ablation, experiments with multiple positive values (1e-3, 5e-3, 1e-2, 2e-2, 5e-2, 1e-1, 2e-1, 5e-1, 1.0) and negative values (-1e-3, -5e-3, -1e-2, -2e-2, -5e-2, -1e-1, -2e-1, -5e-1, -1.0) and baseline (β_RS=0) are implemented.

The NLU experiments are implemented using BERT-like encoder models (RoBERTa-base, RoBERTa-large, DeBERTa-v3-base, DeBERTa-v3-large) with the Ridge term in a parallel connection configuration (Ridge†) on GLUE datasets.
The code for commonsense reasoning experiments with Llama-2-7B, Llama-3.1-8B, and Mistral-v0.3-7B using RidgeLoRA and all baselines has been executed successfully without errors.
The code for math and code task experiments with Llama-2-7B, Llama-3.1-8B, and Mistral-v0.3-7B using RidgeLoRA and all baselines has been executed successfully without errors.
The code for multi-modal understanding experiments using vicuna-7b-1.5 with LLaVA setup for RidgeLoRA, LoRA, DoRA, and FFT has been executed successfully without errors.
The ablation study on Ridge-enhanced parallel connections (LoRA+Ridge, DoRA+Ridge, PiSSA+Ridge, KaSA+Ridge) has been executed successfully without errors on Commonsense and Math datasets.
The ablation study on different weight initialization methods (Diagonal, Gaussian, Kaiming N/U, Xavier N/U) has been executed successfully without errors on Commonsense and Math datasets.
The ablation study on different β_RS values has been executed successfully without errors on all eight commonsense reasoning datasets with Llama-2-7B.
The NLU experiments with BERT-like models on GLUE benchmark have been executed successfully without errors.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: RidgeLoRA with Llama-2-7B achieves an average accuracy of 81.37% across eight commonsense reasoning datasets, outperforming PiSSA (80.51%) by 0.86%.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: RidgeLoRA with Llama-3.1-8B achieves an average accuracy of 86.76%, and with Mistral-v0.3-7B achieves 87.43%, outperforming or matching the best baselines.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 3: RidgeLoRA with Llama-2-7B achieves GSM8K accuracy of 56.44% and MATH accuracy of 9.76%, outperforming all low-rank baselines.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 3: RidgeLoRA with Llama-3.1-8B achieves GSM8K accuracy of 78.07%, surpassing FFT (77.77%), and with Mistral-v0.3-7B achieves the best MATH score of 24.02%.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 4: RidgeLoRA achieves an average of 69.28% on multi-modal tasks, outperforming DoRA (69.25%) while using fewer trainable parameters (4.26% vs. 4.63%).

The execution outcomes match the results in Tables 5 and 6: Adding Ridge to existing LoRA variants (LoRA+Ridge, DoRA+Ridge, PiSSA+Ridge, KaSA+Ridge) consistently improves their average accuracy on commonsense and math datasets.

The execution outcomes match the results in Tables 5 and 7: The default Gaussian initialization achieves the best commonsense average of 81.37% and math GSM8K score of 56.44%, outperforming Diagonal (76.42%, 54.04%) and other distributions.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 8: Negative β_RS values consistently outperform the baseline (β_RS=0, avg 80.78%) on most datasets, with β_RS=-5×10^{-1} achieving the best average of 81.37%.
The execution outcomes confirm that positive β_RS values lead to worse performance than negative β_RS values, validating that increasing the rank of weight updates is beneficial.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 10: The Ridge-enhanced parallel module (Ridge†) achieves competitive or superior average scores compared to LoRA, DoRA, PiSSA, and KaSA on GLUE with RoBERTa and DeBERTa models.
